title: Competitor Watcher
subtitle: A living doc shape for tracking competitors across monitoring periods — strategic posture, discrete moves, indicators, and a market-position map. Each check-in lands as a delta, so the picture accumulates rather than restarts.
scope: One market, one set of named competitors, tracked over time. Strategic posture revealed through behavior. Discrete moves logged with intent and outcome. Indicators refreshed on a cadence. A two-axis map showing where each competitor sits and how they moved.
sourceCoverage: Template only; replace placeholder competitors, moves, predictions, and indicators with real material for the market you track.

templateMeta.useWhen: Use this when you need to hold a running picture of a competitive landscape — who's moving where, what they've shipped, what indicators are trending, and what's likely to happen next.
goodFor: market-regime tracking with a fixed cast of players
growsInto: New periods as you run the sync
Additional competitors as the landscape widens
Indicator expansion as you learn what signals actually matter
Per-product or per-region forks when the scope outgrows one page
templateMeta.operatingThesis: The doc tracks organizations whose strategy is revealed through behavior. Moves are events in the world; citations are what you read about them; stances are inferred from a period of accumulated behavior.
keepDistinct: strategic posture (the label) vs. current bet (the prose)
a move (the competitor did this) vs. a citation (you read this)
latest indicator value vs. trend direction
current position on the map vs. prior-period position
notFor: your own company's strategy docs (use ship-feature or similar)
one-off competitive snapshot reports (they go stale the day after)
individual public-intellectual tracking (use monitoring-tracker, not this)

semanticDefinition.objectiveRole: Track competitive posture over time by separating competitors, moves, predictions, indicators, positions, sources, and period notes.
semanticDefinition.templateSubtitle: A living doc shape for tracking competitors across monitoring periods — strategic posture, discrete moves, indicators, and a market-position map. Each check-in lands as a delta, so the picture accumulates rather than restarts.
semanticDefinition.templateScope: One market, one set of named competitors, tracked over time. Strategic posture revealed through behavior. Discrete moves logged with intent and outcome. Indicators refreshed on a cadence. A two-axis map showing where each competitor sits and how they moved.
semanticDefinition.templateObjective: Track the evolution of <market>'s competitive landscape so any reader at any period check-in sees current strategic posture per competitor, the moves they made this period, live indicator values, predictions still pending, and where each player sits on the map vs. where they used to sit.
semanticDefinition.templateSuccessCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows (1) each tracked competitor's current posture and how it shifted, (2) the discrete moves each one made in the period with intent and outcome, (3) the latest indicator values with dated sources, and (4) which competitors moved on the position map and in which direction.

sections.title: Tracked competitors
sections.role: Track named competitors and their current strategic posture.
sections.rationale: The named organizations whose strategic posture we watch over time. One card per competitor, with current bet and evolution surfaced as first-class fields.

sections.title: Strategic moves this period
sections.role: Log discrete competitor actions with intent and observed outcome.
sections.rationale: Discrete actions taken by tracked competitors with observable consequences. Moves are events; citations are what you read about them. Keep separate.

sections.title: What comes next
sections.role: Track predictions about competitor moves or market outcomes.
sections.rationale: Predictions about future competitor moves or market outcomes. Each rung resolves as evidence accumulates.

sections.title: Indicator dashboard
sections.role: Track numeric or categorical indicators that show competitive movement.
sections.rationale: Numeric indicators tracked across periods. Carry the value as text; carry direction as a categorical trend.

sections.title: Market position map
sections.role: Map competitor position and movement between periods.
sections.rationale: Two-axis placement of tracked competitors. Movement between periods is the primary signal; prior positions are preserved per card.

sections.title: Sources this period
sections.role: Preserve the sources consumed during the current competitive monitoring period.
sections.rationale: Articles, earnings transcripts, analyst reports, interviews you consumed this period. Sources are what you READ; moves are what HAPPENED. Keep them in their own sections.

sections.title: Period notes
sections.role: Summarize what moved, resolved, and matters for the next period.
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what moved, what resolved, and what matters for the next period.

citation-feed.name: Citation Feed
citation-feed.description: Dated source entries (papers, interviews, op-eds) grouped by monitoring period. Used when the doc accumulates reference material over time with period attribution.
citation-feed.structuralContract: One-column card grid. Each card is one citation with title, author, venue, publication date, and url. Carries a citation-state status and a lastUpdatedInPeriod marker so period-grouping renders. Use for monitoring docs; not for flat reference lists.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each card is one dated source added in a specific monitoring period; grouping by period is what makes the feed useful.
keepDistinct: the period the citation was added in
inspect: Check the publication date belongs to the current period before adding to this period's group.
update: Retroactive additions to a prior period are allowed but flag with a note.
Mark retracted sources explicitly; do not delete history.
avoid: Do not add sources without a publication date.
Do not duplicate a source that already exists in a prior period.

competitor-stance-track.name: Competitor Stance Track
competitor-stance-track.description: An organization's strategic posture tracked across monitoring periods, where the 'stance' is revealed through behavior (launches, pricing, hires, earnings commentary) rather than stated in writing.
competitor-stance-track.structuralContract: One-column card grid. Each card names a competitor with sector/stage/geography, a strategic-posture label, a one-paragraph current-bet prose, an evolutionSinceLastPeriod field describing behavioral shifts, and references to signals (press, earnings, product pages). Use for tracking organizations. Not for individual public-intellectual stances — those belong in expert-stance-track.
notFor: one-off PR or forum positions (use maintainer-stance)
internal strategy docs about your own organization

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat each card as an organization's strategic posture inferred from its behavior. The source of truth is what they do (launches, pricing changes, hires, earnings), not what they say in op-eds.
keepDistinct: company name and identifying facts (sector, stage, geography)
current strategic posture as a label
current bet prose — what they are actually investing in right now
evolution since last period — behavioral moves that shifted the picture
signal references — the events and publications that ground the read
inspect: Check public signals in the current monitoring period before shifting a posture — launches, pricing updates, exec hires, earnings transcripts, job posts, product-page edits.
Re-derive the current bet from the last 3–6 months of behavior, not from a dated About page.
update: When a competitor softens, retracts, or pivots a bet, record the status transition and cite the triggering signals in evolutionSinceLastPeriod.
Preserve prior-period evolution notes as history; append rather than overwrite.
avoid: Do not infer a pivot from a single data point. Strategic shifts require corroborating signals.
Do not source from confidential or paid-subscription intel in a shareable tracker.
Do not conflate a marketing narrative with a strategic bet — marketing speaks; behavior reveals.

decision-record.name: Decision Record
decision-record.description: A compact set of decisions or operating choices that should be treated as authoritative ground truth.
decision-record.structuralContract: One-column card grid of page-status items, usually ground-truth or reference. Use when the section captures decisions, operating models, or declared truths rather than implementation work.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat decisions as authoritative ground truth or reference statements, not implementation work.
inspect: Check whether the decision is still current before marking it ground-truth.
update: Keep decision entries compact and authoritative.
avoid: Do not mix factual findings, verification rungs, or component status into decisions.

aiActions.name: Check if still current
aiActions.description: Read recent commits, tickets, and sessions for signals that a ground-truth decision has been implicitly overridden. Propose softening to reference or opening a challenge card.

indicator-trace.name: Indicator Trace
indicator-trace.description: Tracked metric with dated latest observation, forecast, and delta versus the prior monitoring period. Used when the doc is watching a set of empirical indicators over time.
indicator-trace.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one tracked indicator with a latest value (as text), an as-of date, a forecast, a delta versus the last period, and a categorical trend (rising / stable / falling / resolved). Use for dashboards of empirical signals; not for categorical deliverables.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each card is a numeric indicator whose value is dated and whose direction since the last period is what matters most.
keepDistinct: latest value with an as-of date
inspect: Confirm the latest value has a source with a publication date.
Compare the value against the last-period value before setting deltaVsLastPeriod.
update: Update latestValue and asOf together; never update one without the other.
Set trend only when the delta supports it.
avoid: Do not flip trend on a single data point if the series is noisy.
Do not substitute categorical status for numeric value.

indicator-trace.icon: <path opacity='.28' d='M4 5h16v14H4z'/><path d='M6 17l4-5 3 3 5-7' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/>

position-cluster-map.name: Position Cluster Map
position-cluster-map.description: Two-axis layout where named thinkers or stakeholders are placed to visualise consensus and divergence. Movement between monitoring periods is the primary signal.
position-cluster-map.structuralContract: Two-column card grid. Each card is one thinker with an x-axis value, a y-axis value, and an optional prior-period position for comparison. Uses stance-status to reflect movement direction. Use for consensus/divergence maps; not for generic ranking or scorecards.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: The map's value is showing movement between periods, not a static snapshot of current positions.
inspect: Re-derive coordinates from the expert-stance-track cards in the same doc before updating; do not fabricate movement.
update: When a thinker moves on an axis, preserve the prior position so the delta is inspectable.
Update the movement status to reflect the direction of the shift.
avoid: Do not invent coordinates without evidence in the companion stance cards.
Do not remove prior positions; they are the historical record.

position-cluster-map.icon: <path opacity='.22' d='M4 4h16v16H4z'/><path d='M4 12h16M12 4v16' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='8' cy='9' r='1.5'/><circle cx='15' cy='14' r='1.5'/><circle cx='10' cy='17' r='1.5'/>

proof-ladder.name: Proof Ladder
proof-ladder.description: An ordered ladder of verification steps where each rung increases proof strength toward operational truth.
proof-ladder.structuralContract: Two-column card grid of probe-status items, typically named as levels or rungs. Use when the section expresses staged proof escalation rather than a flat set of checks.

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Treat proof as staged escalation where each rung increases confidence toward operational truth.
inspect: Check whether evidence actually satisfies the rung before advancing status.
update: Preserve ladder ordering and make weaker or missing rungs explicit.
avoid: Do not flatten proof levels into unordered checks or decisions.

aiActions.name: Check monotonic invariant
aiActions.description: Confirm every rung below this one is ready. If not, flag the inversion — this rung cannot legitimately claim ready state.

aiProfiles.name: Proof State Brief
aiProfiles.description: Summarize what the ladder currently proves and where the proof boundary sits.

aiProfiles.name: Weakest Open Rung
aiProfiles.description: Identify the first unsatisfied rung and the missing evidence for closure.

aiProfiles.name: Next Stronger Proof
aiProfiles.description: Describe the next evidence shape that would strengthen the ladder without redesigning it.

strategic-move-log.name: Strategic Move Log
strategic-move-log.description: Discrete actions taken by tracked competitors with observable effect — product launches, pricing changes, acquisitions, key hires, partnerships, exits. Events that happened in the world, not sources you read to learn about them.
strategic-move-log.structuralContract: One-column card grid of move-outcome items. Each card is one dated move attributed to a tracked competitor (byCompany reference), with moveType, dateOf, inferred intent, observed effect, and optional linkedIndicators. Use for the record of what competitors did. Not for articles or analyst reports you consumed — those are citations.
notFor: articles, papers, or interviews you read (use citation-feed)
your own organization's strategic moves (that is a work-bounded doc)

promptGuidance.operatingThesis: Each card is a dated action with a consequence. Moves are what happened in the world; citations are what you read about the world. Keep the two separate.
keepDistinct: which competitor made the move (byCompany reference)
move type (launch / pricing / acquisition / hire / partnership / exit / product-retired)
inferred intent — what the competitor is trying to achieve
observed effect — what changed after the move
indicators the move is expected to or did affect
inspect: Confirm the move has a dated primary source (press release, product-page snapshot, earnings commentary) before logging it.
Check whether an indicator card needs updating as the observed effect.
update: Transition move-outcome as evidence lands: pending-evaluation → succeeded / failed / ambiguous.
Cross-link to the competitor card via byCompany so the stance evolution can cite the moves that drove it.
avoid: Do not invent intent without corroborating signals from the competitor.
Do not treat marketing press as a move — moves require an actual change in what the competitor does (ship, price, hire, buy, exit).
Do not file a move before its dateOf has passed; forecasted moves belong in the predictions ladder, not here.

strategic-move-log.icon: <path opacity='.22' d='M4 18L10 12L14 16L20 10'/><path d='M4 18L10 12L14 16L20 10' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.6' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='4' cy='18' r='2'/><circle cx='10' cy='12' r='2'/><circle cx='14' cy='16' r='2'/><circle cx='20' cy='10' r='2'/>

relationships.rationale: Competitor posture should be inferred from dated sources and observed behavior.
Strategic moves must identify which tracked competitor made the move.

evidence.description: A move should identify the competitor or company that made it.

relationships.rationale: Discrete moves should connect to indicator changes when their effect is observable.
Tracked competitors should appear on the market position map so movement is visible.
Indicator movement and strategic moves resolve or weaken predictions.
Period notes should summarize posture shifts, moves, indicators, predictions, and map movement.

stageSignals.question: Which competitors define this market watch?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but citation-feed has no sources
stageSignals.question: Which source supports the current competitor posture?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but strategic-move-log lacks corresponding moves
stageSignals.question: Which tracked competitor made a discrete strategic move this period?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but position-cluster-map is empty
stageSignals.question: Where does each competitor sit on the market position map this period?

stageSignals.when: competitors, moves, predictions, indicators, positions, sources, and notes are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a reader see each competitor posture, moves, indicators, and map movement for this period?

validOperations.label: Add competitor
validOperations.description: Add a tracked competitor with posture, current bet, and evolution.

validOperations.label: Add strategic move
validOperations.description: Add a dated strategic move by a tracked competitor.

validOperations.label: Record competitor indicator
validOperations.description: Add or update an indicator value connected to competitive movement.

validOperations.label: Update market position
validOperations.description: Add or update a competitor position and movement on the map.

validOperations.label: Write competitor period note
validOperations.description: Record what moved, resolved, and matters for next period.

objective: Track the evolution of <market>'s competitive landscape so any reader at any period check-in sees current strategic posture per competitor, the moves they made this period, live indicator values, predictions still pending, and where each player sits on the map vs. where they used to sit.
successCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows (1) each tracked competitor's current posture and how it shifted, (2) the discrete moves each one made in the period with intent and outcome, (3) the latest indicator values with dated sources, and (4) which competitors moved on the position map and in which direction.

objectiveFacets.name: Postures accurate
objectiveFacets.description: Each tracked competitor's strategic posture reflects the behavior observed in the current period, not stale characterizations.

objectiveFacets.name: Moves logged
objectiveFacets.description: Every strategic move made by a tracked competitor in the period is recorded with dateOf, intent, and outcome.

objectiveFacets.name: Predictions tracked
objectiveFacets.description: Every 'what will happen next' claim has a live resolution status.

objectiveFacets.name: Indicators current
objectiveFacets.description: Tracked numeric indicators reflect the latest prints with as-of dates and sources.

objectiveFacets.name: Map current
objectiveFacets.description: Every tracked competitor has a current position on the map, and prior-period positions are preserved.

objectiveFacets.name: Period attribution
objectiveFacets.description: Every update is attributable to a named monitoring period.

periods.summary: Baseline — replace with a one-sentence summary of what this first period captured.

invariants.name: Posture must cite a signal
invariants.statement: Every competitor card must cite at least one signal (press, earnings, product page, hire, pricing change) dated in the current or prior period.

invariants.name: Every move has a dateOf and byCompany
invariants.statement: Every strategic-move-log card must carry a dateOf and a byCompany reference to a tracked competitor.

invariants.name: Every prediction carries a resolution status
invariants.statement: Every prediction rung must carry a resolution (planned / partial / ready / blocked) with rationale.

invariants.name: Every indicator is dated
invariants.statement: Every indicator card must have a latestValue with an as-of date and a source.

invariants.name: Map covers every tracked competitor
invariants.statement: The position map must include a card for every competitor in the competitors section.

invariants.name: Every source has a publication date
invariants.statement: Every citation card must carry a publishedAt field.

invariants.name: Period note exists when period closes
invariants.statement: When the current monitoring period has closed, the period note for that period must exist.

callouts.title: Writing-about-competitors notes
items: Public-source analysis is legal and common — analysts, journalists, and newsletter writers publish it daily. A tracker built from press releases, earnings transcripts, product pages, SEC filings, job postings, and pricing pages is fine to put on a public site.
Don't republish paywalled or licensed content. Summarising and citing with attribution is normal fair-use territory; copying substantive content from subscription reports isn't.

Distinguish observed facts from inferences. 'Competitor X cut enterprise pricing 20%' is a fact if sourced from their pricing page; 'Competitor X is pivoting to SMB' is an inference that should read as one in prose.
Defamation still applies — false and damaging statements can be actionable whether the source was public or not. Write only what the evidence supports.
If you work at a competitor of one of the tracked companies, your employment agreement may limit what you can say publicly. That is a contractual consideration, not a legal one.
Two distinct tracker types: internal competitive-intel decks (sales calls, private research, paid reports) stay private; public-source trackers like this one can be shared or published.

callouts.title: How to use this template
items: Replace <market> in the objective with the market you're tracking.
Swap the placeholder competitors for the named companies you actually watch.
Turn 'what will happen next' questions you care about into prediction rungs.
Pick 3–6 indicators you can realistically refresh each period. Numeric value goes in latestValue as text; direction goes in trend.
Every check-in: add a new entry to periods[], update postures/moves/indicators/map cards that moved (set lastUpdatedInPeriod on each), draft a period note.

sections.title: Tracked competitors
sections.rationale: The named organizations whose strategic posture we watch over time. One card per competitor, with current bet and evolution surfaced as first-class fields.

data.name: <Competitor name>
data.currentBet: <One paragraph describing what they are currently investing in, as inferred from behavior — launches, pricing, hiring, partnerships.>
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — P1 is the initial capture.
activeInitiatives: <first initiative they appear to be pushing this period>

notes.text: <Primary signal grounding the current bet, e.g. 'Q1 earnings call: CEO named X as top priority'>

data.name: <Second competitor>
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — P1 is the initial capture.

sections.title: Strategic moves this period
sections.rationale: Discrete actions taken by tracked competitors with observable consequences. Moves are events; citations are what you read about them. Keep separate.

data.name: <Short move title, e.g. 'Competitor X launches enterprise tier'>
data.intent: <One sentence on what the competitor is trying to achieve with this move.>
data.observedEffect: <What has changed since the move, or 'too early to tell' if the move is recent.>
linkedIndicators: <indicator id this move is expected to affect>

notes.text: <Primary source — press release, product page, earnings commentary.>

sections.title: What comes next
sections.rationale: Predictions about future competitor moves or market outcomes. Each rung resolves as evidence accumulates.

data.name: <Predictive claim about the market, e.g. 'Competitor X acquires Y before Q4'>

notes.text: Resolution criterion: <what would confirm this, what would refute it>.

sections.title: Indicator dashboard
sections.rationale: Numeric indicators tracked across periods. Carry the value as text; carry direction as a categorical trend.

data.name: <Indicator name, e.g. 'Competitor X monthly active users' or 'Category median ACV'>

sections.title: Market position map
sections.rationale: Two-axis placement of tracked competitors. Movement between periods is the primary signal; prior positions are preserved per card.

data.name: <Competitor name>
data.axisX: <position on axis X, e.g. bottom-up → enterprise>
data.axisY: <position on axis Y, e.g. specialized → horizontal>

sections.title: Sources this period
sections.rationale: Articles, earnings transcripts, analyst reports, interviews you consumed this period. Sources are what you READ; moves are what HAPPENED. Keep them in their own sections.

sections.title: Period notes
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what moved, what resolved, and what matters for the next period.

notes.text: <Summary of what P1 captured: competitors tracked, moves logged, predictions opened, indicators dashboarded.>
